source_paper_title: A Superlinearly Convergent Subgradient Method for Sharp Semismooth Problems

context:
Section 5.2.2 (Max-linear regression and PolyakSGM), page 38

We do not attempt to verify assumptions (A1) and (A2) for this problem class. Instead, we note that if the solution set is isolated, semialgebraicity of \(f\) implies that we (A2) holds (see Proposition 2.1). On the other hand, verifying Assumption (A1) is remains an intriguing problem.
title: Verify sharp growth constant for max-linear regression with absolute loss setting
background_latex:
Let \(a_1,\dots,a_m\in\mathbb{R}^d\) be given measurement vectors and let \(\bar y\in\mathbb{R}^m\) be given observations. For an integer \(r\ge 1\), define parameters \(\beta=(\beta_1,\dots,\beta_r)\) with \(\beta_j\in\mathbb{R}^d\). Define the max-linear predictor
\[
\hat y_i(\beta):=\max_{j\in\{1,\dots,r\}}\langle \beta_j,a_i\rangle,\qquad i=1,\dots,m.
\]
Consider the nonsmooth objective
\[
f(\beta):=\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^m \bigl|\bar y_i-\hat y_i(\beta)\bigr|.
\]
Let \(f^*:=\inf_{\beta} f(\beta)\) and \(X^*:=\operatorname{argmin} f\) denote the optimal value and set of minimizers (assumed nonempty). For \(x\) in a Euclidean space, \(\mathrm{dist}(x,X^*):=\inf_{z\in X^*}\|x-z\|\).
problem_definition_latex:
Determine conditions (and/or prove in the intended statistical model) under which there exists \(\mu>0\) and a neighborhood \(U\) of a minimizer \(\bar\beta\in X^*\) such that the sharp growth (error bound) inequality
\[
f(\beta)-f^*\ge \mu\,\mathrm{dist}(\beta,X^*)\qquad\text{for all }\beta\in U
\]
holds for the max-linear regression objective \(f\) defined above.
latex:
Let \(a_1,\dots,a_m\in\mathbb{R}^d\) be given measurement vectors and let \(\bar y\in\mathbb{R}^m\) be given observations. For an integer \(r\ge 1\), define parameters \(\beta=(\beta_1,\dots,\beta_r)\) with \(\beta_j\in\mathbb{R}^d\). Define the max-linear predictor
\[
\hat y_i(\beta):=\max_{j\in\{1,\dots,r\}}\langle \beta_j,a_i\rangle,\qquad i=1,\dots,m.
\]
Consider the nonsmooth objective
\[
f(\beta):=\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^m \bigl|\bar y_i-\hat y_i(\beta)\bigr|.
\]
Let \(f^*:=\inf_{\beta} f(\beta)\) and \(X^*:=\operatorname{argmin} f\) denote the optimal value and set of minimizers (assumed nonempty). For \(x\) in a Euclidean space, \(\mathrm{dist}(x,X^*):=\inf_{z\in X^*}\|x-z\|\).

Determine conditions (and/or prove in the intended statistical model) under which there exists \(\mu>0\) and a neighborhood \(U\) of a minimizer \(\bar\beta\in X^*\) such that the sharp growth (error bound) inequality
\[
f(\beta)-f^*\ge \mu\,\mathrm{dist}(\beta,X^*)\qquad\text{for all }\beta\in U
\]
holds for the max-linear regression objective \(f\) defined above.
open_problem_quote: We do not attempt to verify assumptions (A1) and (A2) for this problem class. Instead, we note that if the solution set is isolated, semialgebraicity of \(f\) implies that we (A2) holds (see Proposition 2.1). On the other hand, verifying Assumption (A1) is remains an intriguing problem.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: Sharpness can be empirically tested on finite-dimensional instances by approximating minimizers and checking local ratios \((f(\beta)-f^*)/\mathrm{dist}(\beta,X^*)\), but computing \(\mathrm{dist}(\beta,X^*)\) and certifying \(f^*\) and \(X^*\) is itself nontrivial. Automated searches might still find instances where sharpness fails or appears to hold with small \(\mu\).
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: A coding-agent loop could generate random designs \(a_i\) and ground-truth parameters, numerically solve for \(X^*\), and optimize over perturbations to estimate lower bounds on the sharpness modulus \(\mu\) or find apparent violations. The fitness signal (estimated minimal local ratio) is computable but noisy and sensitive to numerical optimization accuracy.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The objective in the open problem,
\[f(\beta)=\tfrac1m\sum_{i=1}^m\bigl|\bar y_i-\max_{j\le r}\langle \beta_j,a_i\rangle\bigr|,\]
is a locally Lipschitz, semialgebraic, piecewise-linear function of \(\beta\). In the framework of Charisopoulos and Davis, semialgebraicity strongly suggests that the semismoothness-type condition they call (b)-regularity should hold automatically (at least along isolated minimizers, and often along solution sets after stratification). Hence the main missing ingredient for applying their superlinear subgradient/bundle methodology to max-linear regression is precisely the sharp growth (error bound) inequality
\[f(\beta)-f^*\ge \mu\,\mathrm{dist}(\beta,X^*)\quad\text{locally near }X^*.
\]

Existing literature does not appear to directly settle sharpness for this specific nonconvex max-linear absolute-loss model in the intended statistical setting (e.g., random designs and a planted max-linear ground truth). However, there is a mature variational-analytic toolkit for proving error bounds for piecewise-linear/inequality systems via metric regularity and polyhedral geometry (e.g., Lewis--Pang and related work, as well as the general theory in Rockafellar--Wets). A plausible route is to rewrite the model with auxiliary variables \(t_i\) representing \(\max_j\langle \beta_j,a_i\rangle\) and residual variables for the absolute values, yielding a polyhedral system coupled with the nonconvex assignment structure (which index \(j\) is active at each \(i\)). If one can show that, in a neighborhood of a target minimizer \(\bar\beta\), the active pattern (or a suitable set of active patterns) is stable and the resulting local model is effectively polyhedral with a nondegenerate solution (a strong form of constraint qualification/metric regularity), then a local Lipschitz error bound—and hence sharp growth—follows.

At present, the problem should be regarded as open: sharp growth for max-linear regression with absolute loss likely requires additional identifiability and nondegeneracy conditions (e.g., separation/margin conditions preventing ties in the max, and a local uniqueness/modulo-permutation structure of \(X^*\)), together with design assumptions ensuring a polyhedral error bound constant \(\mu\) bounded away from zero with high probability. Promising directions include: (i) proving metric subregularity of the KKT mapping for an epigraph reformulation at \(\bar\beta\); (ii) establishing a local “active-set stability” or strict complementarity-type condition that reduces the model to a fixed piecewise-linear regime; and (iii) deriving probabilistic lower bounds on the resulting Hoffman-type constants under random \(a_i\).

sources.title: A superlinearly convergent subgradient method for sharp semismooth problems
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops SuperPolyak, a locally superlinearly convergent subgradient/bundle-type method for minimizing locally Lipschitz objectives satisfying (i) sharp growth (an error bound) and (ii) (b)-regularity (a uniform semismoothness estimate). Provides calculus rules implying (b)-regularity for broad semialgebraic constructions and includes max-linear regression among numerical illustrations/problem classes motivating the sharpness assumption.
sources.connection: The open problem is exactly to verify the sharp growth inequality for the max-linear absolute-loss objective. This paper supplies the algorithmic motivation and identifies sharpness as the missing regularity condition; it also suggests (b)-regularity is likely automatic here because the objective is semialgebraic/piecewise linear, focusing attention on proving sharp growth under statistical assumptions.

sources.title: Stochastic subgradient method converges at the rate O(1/k) on sharp weakly convex functions
sources.theorem_or_result: Establishes improved convergence guarantees for (stochastic) subgradient methods on sharp weakly convex objectives, relating sharp growth/error bounds to algorithmic rates and to variational-analytic regularity (e.g., metric subregularity of the subdifferential).
sources.connection: Provides general variational-analytic machinery for proving and exploiting sharpness. For max-linear absolute loss, it motivates proving a local error bound via metric subregularity/tilt-stability style conditions for piecewise-linear objectives.

sources.title: Error bounds for convex inequality systems
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops a theory of local/global error bounds for convex inequality systems, connecting them to constraint qualifications and metric regularity. Provides tools to derive Lipschitz error bounds for piecewise-linear/convex systems and to compute/estimate constants.
sources.connection: Although the max-linear absolute-loss objective is generally nonconvex in \(\beta\) for \(r>1\), it is piecewise linear and can be represented with auxiliary variables and linear inequalities. This paper is a foundational source for deriving local error bounds from polyhedral geometry/metric regularity, which is a plausible route to proving sharp growth in neighborhoods where the active pattern is stable.

sources.title: A global error bound for convex inequalities
sources.theorem_or_result: Provides global error bounds for systems of convex inequalities under suitable conditions, with emphasis on polyhedral/convex-analytic structure and explicit constants in certain cases.
sources.connection: Max-linear models admit epigraph/inequality representations using auxiliary variables \(t_i\ge \langle \beta_j,a_i\rangle\). While the full objective is nonconvex for \(r>1\), local analysis near a minimizer often reduces to a fixed active pattern yielding a polyhedral model; global/local error-bound techniques for convex inequalities inform how to obtain a sharp growth constant \(\mu\) from such representations.

sources.title: Variational Analysis
sources.theorem_or_result: Comprehensive reference on variational analysis, including metric regularity/subregularity, error bounds, and stability properties for nonsmooth and set-valued mappings.
sources.connection: Sharp growth is an error-bound property that can often be established via metric subregularity of an associated mapping (e.g., subdifferential or KKT system of an epigraph reformulation). This monograph provides the core tools and definitions needed to formalize such an approach for the max-linear absolute-loss objective.
